region_of_origin: China (Xinjiang / Northwest China), served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for flame-grilled lamb skewers; closest_parent 'lamb meat food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (meat from sheep/lamb, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific skewer or flame-grilled lamb code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of flaming lamb skewers consists of 3 to 4 skewers, each holding 2–3 pieces of marinated lamb, totalling approximately 150 g of cooked lamb meat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, ground, cooked, broiled (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 174351); supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entry for lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for flame-grilled lamb skewers exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. USDA FoodData Central data for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351) was used as the closest defensible analogue for cooked lamb meat. Values reflect grilled/broiled lamb without added sauces; spice additions (cumin, chilli) contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical usage levels. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical seasoning (salt, soy-free marinade) consistent with street-food preparation style.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pure Protein and Fat Foods: Meat, poultry, and fish have negligible glycemic response; University of Sydney GI Database — meat category reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure cooked lamb meat contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008) confirm that meat and poultry products with zero carbohydrate content have an effective GI of 0. Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 480 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation to stay within the 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Flaming lamb skewers contain virtually no digestible carbohydrate, producing a negligible glycemic response, making them a suitable high-protein option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 150 g serving provides approximately 10.4 g saturated fat and 146 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency and pair with vegetables to offset the saturated fat load within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flaming Lamb Skewers (火焰羊肉串, huǒyàn yángròu chuàn) are a popular Chinese street-food dish originating from the Xinjiang and Northwest Chinese culinary tradition, widely served across China and in Chinese restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of marinated lamb threaded onto metal or bamboo skewers and cooked over an open flame or charcoal grill at high heat, often with a dramatic flaming presentation that caramelises the exterior while keeping the interior juicy. The lamb is typically marinated with cumin, dried chilli flakes, salt, garlic, and a light coating of oil — a spice profile characteristic of Uyghur and Lanzhou-style grilling. The result is a smoky, slightly charred, aromatic meat with a tender and slightly chewy texture. Nutritionally, flaming lamb skewers are a high-protein, moderate-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content. Per 100 g of cooked lamb, the dish provides approximately 258 kcal, 25.6 g protein, 16.7 g fat (including 6.9 g saturated fat), and 0 g carbohydrate. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and when prepared with halal-certified lamb, it is suitable for Muslim consumers. Clinically, the negligible carbohydrate content makes it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the moderate saturated fat and sodium content warrant attention for those managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Chinese noodle restaurants and hawker-style eateries, including Lanzhou beef noodle establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb skewers?
Are lamb skewers suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for flaming lamb skewers was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked lamb data (FDC ID 174351), which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical street-food seasoning. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact preparation and marinade composition of the field survey restaurant version are unknown.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lamb meat with no carbohydrate-containing marinade ingredients at significant quantity. This is naturally zero for plain cooked lamb. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero for plain grilled lamb. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a pure meat product. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lamb. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lamb. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351). vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351). 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention, consistent with the Veritas sentinel rule for zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. 4. FoodOn match is closest_parent (FOODON_03302051, lamb meat food product); no exact FoodOn term for flame-grilled lamb skewers exists. FoodEx2 match is closest_parent (A00LC, meat from sheep/lamb cooked); no specific skewer preparation code exists. SNOMED CT match is none; no suitable prepared-dish concept found. 5. Halal flag set to true as lamb skewers are inherently halal when prepared with halal-certified lamb, which is standard practice at the referenced Lanzhou-style restaurant. Gluten-free flag set to true as the standard preparation uses no wheat-based ingredients. 6. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g is an upward adaptation from the USDA base value to reflect typical salt and seasoning applied during street-food grilling; this is documented in nutrient_source.source_match_notes.
